message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec id="s21">
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Population-level viral load distribution, measured by cycle threshold (Ct), has been demonstrated to enable real-time estimation of R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    for SARS-CoV-2 ancestral strain. Generalisability of the framework under different circulating variants and pre-existing immunity remains unclear.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s22">
                  <jats:title>Aim</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study aimed to examine the impact of evolving variants and population immunity on the generalizability of Ct-based transmission estimation framework.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s23">
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We obtained the first Ct record of local COVID-19 cases from July 2020 to January 2023 in Hong Kong. We modeled the association between daily viral load distribution and the conventional estimates of R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    based on case count. We trained the model using data from wave 3 (i.e., ancestral strain with minimal population immunity) and predicted R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    for wave 5, 6 and 7 (i.e., omicron subvariants with > 70% vaccine coverage). Cross-validation was performed by training on the other 4 waves. Stratification analysis by disease severity was conducted to evaluate the impact of the changing severity profiles.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s24">
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Trained with the ancestral dominated wave 3, our model provided accurate estimation of R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    , with the area under the ROC curve of 0.98 (95% confidence interval: 0.96, 1.00), 0.62 (95% CI: 0.53, 0.70) and 0.80 (95% CI: 0.73, 0.88) for three omicron dominated waves 5 to 7, respectively. Models trained on the other four waves also had high accuracy. Stratification analysis suggested potential impact of case severity on model estimation, which coincided with the fluctuation of sampling delay.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s25">
                  <jats:title>Discussion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our findings suggested that incorporating population viral shedding can provide accurate real-time estimation of transmission with evolving variants and population immunity. Application of the model needs to account for sampling delay.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Effective real-time transmission estimations incorporating population viral load distributions amid SARS-CoV-2 variants and pre-existing immunity

reference.unstructured: The Government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region. COVID-19 & Flu express. Local Situation of COVID-19 Activity (as of October 21, 2022)., <https://www.chp.gov.hk/files/pdf/local_situation_covid19_en_20221021.pdf> (2022).
The Government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region. Latest situation of COVID-19 (as of August 31, 2022). <https://www.chp.gov.hk/files/pdf/local_situation_covid19_en.pdf > (2022).

The Government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region. COVID-19 & Flu express. Local Situation of COVID-19 Activity (as of February 8, 2023)., <https://www.chp.gov.hk/en/resources/29/100148.html> (2023).
